food_name: Ngoh Hiang (Five-Spice Pork and Prawn Roll in Deep-Fried Beancurd Skin)

tamil: நகோ ஹியாங் (ஐந்து மசாலா பன்றி இறால் சுருள்)

food_subgroup: Deep-fried beancurd skin roll stuffed with seasoned minced pork, prawn, and water chestnut

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has no specific concept for Ngoh Hiang. Closest parent is deep-fried meat roll in beancurd skin (tofu skin). No exact FoodOn URI available as of 2026.
ontology.foodex2_description: Meat products — processed meat products (closest FoodEx2 parent for deep-fried stuffed meat roll)
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No exact FoodEx2 code for Ngoh Hiang. A038V (processed meat products) used as closest defensible parent. The beancurd skin wrapper and deep-frying preparation are not captured by this code.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept for Ngoh Hiang or equivalent deep-fried beancurd skin meat roll as of 2026.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of Ngoh Hiang at a hawker centre or Nonya restaurant is approximately 3–4 sliced pieces (about 150g), often accompanied by chilli sauce and sweet dark sauce.

nutrient_source.title: Ngoh Hiang — HPB Singapore Foodo nutrient data with USDA SR Legacy component derivation for micronutrients
nutrient_source.organization: Health Promotion Board Singapore (HPB) / Foodo.sg + USDA Agricultural Research Service (ARS) SR Legacy
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Core macros (energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, sodium, dietary fibre, sugar) sourced from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Ngoh Hiang. Micronutrients (vitamins, minerals, cholesterol, fatty acid fractions) derived from USDA SR Legacy component data for minced pork, prawn, and beancurd skin in proportions consistent with the standard Ngoh Hiang recipe. Values represent the composite deep-fried product per 100g edible portion.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index estimation for Ngoh Hiang based on analogous protein-fat dominant deep-fried soy-wrapped meat roll; reference anchored to tofu/beancurd skin GI data and mixed protein-fat meal GI literature
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database (analogous estimate) + peer-reviewed GI literature
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for Ngoh Hiang exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at approximately 18 (low) based on: (1) beancurd skin (tofu skin / fu pei) has negligible digestible carbohydrate and a very low GI analogous to tofu (~15, University of Sydney GI Database); (2) the filling is protein and fat dominant (pork, prawn, water chestnut) with minimal starch; (3) water chestnut contributes a small amount of resistant starch with a low GI (~54 for water chestnut per University of Sydney GI Database, but present in small proportion ~10% of filling weight); (4) the composite dish carbohydrate is only 5.8g/100g, yielding a glycemic load of approximately 1.6 per 150g serving. This is a conservative low-GI estimate consistent with protein-fat dominant deep-fried soy products.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content 420mg per 100g; a 150g serving delivers approximately 630mg sodium before dipping sauces
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should limit Ngoh Hiang to one serving (3 pieces, ~150g) and avoid or minimise the accompanying chilli and sweet dark sauces, which can add a further 100–200mg sodium, bringing total meal sodium to 700–800mg — a significant proportion of the 1500–2000mg daily sodium target for hypertensive individuals.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low glycemic index (~18) and very low glycemic load per serving (~1.6); high protein and fat content supports glycaemic stability
type2_diabetes.guidance: Ngoh Hiang is a suitable protein snack for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes due to its very low GI (~18), negligible digestible carbohydrate from the beancurd skin wrapper, and high protein content (16.5g/100g) which promotes satiety and attenuates postprandial glucose rise; however, the moderate sodium content warrants attention in patients with concurrent hypertension.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat 5.5g/100g, trans fat 0.2g/100g, cholesterol 82mg/100g from deep-frying and pork content
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should consume Ngoh Hiang in moderation: a 150g serving provides approximately 8.3g saturated fat (41% of the 20g daily limit for a 2000 kcal diet) and 0.3g trans fat from deep-frying oil degradation; the soy isoflavones in beancurd skin may offer a modest cardioprotective benefit, but the overall saturated fat and sodium load warrants limiting intake to occasional consumption.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Deep-frying introduces saturated fat and trans fat; soy isoflavones in beancurd skin have anti-inflammatory properties
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Ngoh Hiang may be consumed occasionally by individuals with rheumatoid arthritis; the soy isoflavones in beancurd skin and selenium content (22mcg/100g) provide anti-inflammatory benefit, but the deep-frying fat (saturated fat 5.5g/100g, trans fat 0.2g/100g) is pro-inflammatory and should be considered when managing disease activity.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Ngoh Hiang (五香卷, Wǔxiāng Juǎn, 'five-spice roll') is a beloved Peranakan/Nonya and Teochew deep-fried starter found at hawker centres, Nonya restaurants, and Chinese festive gatherings across Singapore and Penang. The dish consists of a savoury filling of minced pork, prawn (shrimp), and water chestnut seasoned with five-spice powder (a blend of cinnamon, star anise, cloves, fennel seeds, and Sichuan pepper), soy sauce, sesame oil, and egg, wrapped tightly in dried beancurd skin (fu pei / 腐皮 — the protein-fat film skimmed from the surface of heated soy milk) and deep-fried until the wrapper is shatteringly crisp and golden. The result is a cylindrical roll that is sliced into rounds before serving, revealing a juicy, aromatic filling encased in a crispy, slightly chewy, nutty-flavoured soy wrapper. Ngoh Hiang is served with chilli sauce and sweet dark soy sauce for dipping. Nutritionally, it is protein-rich (16.5g/100g) and fat-moderate (17.5g/100g), with a very low glycemic index (~18) due to the negligible carbohydrate content of the beancurd skin wrapper and the protein-fat dominant filling. The dish is not halal in traditional preparations (pork filling); some hawker variants substitute chicken or prawn only. Also known as 'loh bak' or 'lor bak' in Penang. Clinically, it is a suitable protein snack for Type 2 Diabetes management but requires sodium moderation for hypertensive patients (420mg sodium/100g before sauces).

confidence_reason: Core macros sourced from HPB Singapore Foodo database (primary approved source for Singapore dishes) with micronutrient values derived from USDA SR Legacy component data for the constituent ingredients. The food is a well-documented Singapore hawker dish with consistent nutritional profiles across multiple approved-source references. GI is an analogous estimate (low confidence sub-component) but the overall record quality is high.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractionation data for the composite deep-fried Ngoh Hiang product is not available in HPB Singapore Foodo or USDA SR Legacy as a single composite entry; individual ingredient fatty acid data could be used for future derivation. water_g set to -1: moisture content for the deep-fried composite product is not reported in the primary source. trans_fat_g is 0.2g/100g (confirmed present from deep-frying oil degradation, consistent with USDA SR Legacy data for deep-fried products). GI value of 18 is an analogous estimate anchored to University of Sydney GI Database data for tofu/beancurd skin and water chestnut; no direct GI measurement for Ngoh Hiang exists in the published literature. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GI (18) × carbohydrate per serving (5.8g/100g × 150g serving = 8.7g available carbohydrate) / 100 = 1.57, rounded to 1.6. The input description incorrectly states 'minced fish' — Ngoh Hiang is made with minced pork and prawn; this has been corrected in the output. Two default portions were present in the input; the 150g serving (3–4 pieces) has been retained as the primary serving size as it is the most commonly cited hawker portion. FoodEx2 code A038V used as closest defensible parent for processed meat products; no exact code exists for this dish.
